Mateo:
HERO Driver with Strategist tones [claro]
Mateo's game leans **clearly on action**: it's what they chose in 8 of their 12 decisions. And behind that drive a **Strategist streak** shows through: in several scenes they also chose looking at the plan before acting. Today, the way they show up in the activity runs through there: moving forward, deciding and getting things moving.
# Their mix
In Argo, every profile blends the model's four colors in its own way, and in Mateo's, one ingredient stands out: **action**, which they chose in 8 of their 12 decisions. Close behind appears **the detail and the plan**, which adds nuance to how they play. Connecting with others and supporting the group, on the other hand, weigh less today in how they decide: these are colors they also have available and that will find their place over time.
Faced with the same situation, Mateo tends to asking first "what do I do" before "who do I do it with".
# Their decision pattern
Throughout the game, Mateo **decided at varied paces**: resolved faster right in the choices that go with their main driver, and adjusted their timing on the rest. That coupling between what they choose and how long they take is a very personal signature, and it tells us something lovely: **when something truly excites them, they respond with ease**.
Faced with a decision that excites them, they barely think it over; faced with one outside their comfort zone, they take their time.
# Their engine
In the reaction-and-decision mini-games, Mateo moved with a **nimble pace**: they tend to read quickly and trust their first impulse. They usually **get actions going and resolve things with ease when a decision has to be made on the spot**. Supporting Mateo means giving room for that speed and, little by little, offering chances to discover when it's worth taking one more second.
If something needs to be started, Mateo is usually among the first to get going. Adding the question "what if I look for a second first?" helps Mateo grow without dimming their drive.
# Facing the storm
When things get complicated, Mateo doesn't fall back on a single formula: **under pressure they tend to have more than one resource**. A lean toward **looking at the plan before acting** shows up, without it being their only way out, and that flexibility is a valuable sign: they can adapt to what each moment calls for.
Faced with the unexpected, they will likely first try to understand what's going on and only then act.
# How much the group moves them
Among Mateo's drivers, the bond with the team today **shows up more in the background**, and it's simply part of their recipe at this moment: it says nothing about their social life or their friendships. Their way of adding to the group today runs **more through doing than through the emotional side**, and when their contribution shows, the bond usually comes afterward. A lovely way to bring Mateo closer is to **give them a role where their drive has an impact on the team**.
Inviting Mateo to lead the start of a shared play brings Mateo closer to the group from their strength, without pushing them into a place that isn't theirs today.
# When things go well
When Mateo pulls something off, tends to light up right away toward **the next goal**: enjoys moving forward more than stopping to savor what was achieved. It showed clearly in the game, because on reaching the finish they chose **looking ahead to the next challenge**. That's how they experience it, and that's perfectly fine. Supporting Mateo means helping them also **notice and celebrate what they've achieved** before setting off again.
After a good result, a simple "look at everything you accomplished" helps Mateo give enjoyment its place too.
# When things go wrong
When something doesn't go the way they hoped, Mateo tends to want to **try again right away**, with all their drive: sets their sights on the next attempt more than on what just happened. It's their way of processing it, and it has its worth. Supporting Mateo means **giving them a concrete next step right away** and, little by little, inviting them to take a second to look at what to adjust, without dampening that drive.
When something doesn't go Mateo's way and they already want to go for more, it helps to hear a "I love that you want to keep going, let's try something different next time."
# What lights them up
What tends to light Mateo up most is **having a clear goal and feeling that their drive moves things forward**. When they sense that their initiative leaves a real mark, they truly come alive, and recognizing that impact is often their best fuel.
A "thanks to you getting it going, this worked out" will probably motivate Mateo far more than general praise.
# Words that connect (and the ones that jar)
You start this one off. | What do you suggest to solve it? | Today you set the pace. | Show me how you'd do it.
Wait, it's not your turn yet. | Just do it exactly as I say. | Better stay on the sidelines and watch.
It's not a script, it's a compass for tone: what usually **reaches Mateo most is anything that recognizes their initiative**, and what grates is anything that halts their drive without explaining why. With that in mind, the exact words are up to you.
# Before, during and after
Three moments where a small intention changes a lot.
Offer them a concrete goal and room to take the initiative. Knowing they'll be able to **get something of their own going** focuses and excites Mateo.
If they speed up too much, instead of stopping the momentum, **channel their drive**: acknowledge the eagerness and add a quick read of the situation before going. It's about adding, not shutting down.
Recognize what they **set in motion**, not just the result. For an action-oriented profile, a "you got everything going" usually lands better than general praise.
If there's a new activity on Saturday, telling them "you'll get to start it off" turns the nerves into eagerness.
# A reset that works
When Mateo gets frustrated, waiting around tends to be hard for them, and that's very understandable in an action-oriented profile. What **tends to help most is a small, concrete action** to take right then: a clear task, a short goal, something that brings back the feeling of making progress.
Instead of "calm down", a "take care of this for a minute" usually gets things back on track much better.
# Beyond the sport
This drive to move forward **doesn't live only in sport**. It tends to show up when Mateo organizes a game, suggests plans, or wants to solve something right away. Seeing it in those moments helps you understand it's not impatience: it's **their way of being in the world**.
If at home they're one of the first to say "come on, let's do this", you're seeing the same engine that drives Mateo in the activity.

Sofi plays today with **two well-balanced engines**: supporting the group and action. It's not indecision, quite the opposite: they have two registers and tend to choose according to what each moment calls for.
# Their mix
In Argo, every profile blends the model's four colors in its own way, and in Sofi's, one ingredient stands out: **supporting the group**, which they chose in 5 of their 12 decisions. Close behind appears **action**, which adds nuance to how they play. The detail and the plan and also connecting with others, on the other hand, weigh less today in how they decide: these are colors they also have available and that will find their place over time.
Faced with the same situation, Sofi tends to making sure everyone is okay before starting.
# Their decision pattern
Throughout the game, Sofi **decided at a fairly steady pace**: they kept their commitment from start to finish, without starting out unsure or easing off toward the end. It's a lovely sign of consistency in how they get involved with each choice.
When something matters to them, Sofi tends to keep the same dedication from the first moment to the last.
# Facing the storm
When things get complicated, Sofi doesn't fall back on a single formula: **under pressure they tend to have more than one resource**. A lean toward **moving forward and deciding** shows up, without it being their only way out, and that flexibility is a valuable sign: they can adapt to what each moment calls for.
Faced with the unexpected, they will likely move fast to solve it as soon as possible.
# How much the group moves them
In Sofi's relationship with their group, a **clear need for the group to be in harmony** shows up. These are different social drivers (involving isn't the same as holding together), and knowing which one weighs more helps in supporting Sofi from where they truly feel at ease.
Making room for that social side, to the extent it comes naturally, usually helps Sofi feel part of things.
# When things go well
When Sofi pulls something off, tends to be glad above all that **the team is doing well**, more than about personal credit. It showed clearly in the game, because on reaching the finish they chose **sharing the joy with teammates**. That's how they experience it, and that's perfectly fine. Supporting Sofi means helping them also **notice and celebrate what they've achieved** before setting off again.
After a good result, a simple "look at everything you accomplished" helps Sofi give enjoyment its place too.
# When things go wrong
When something doesn't go the way they hoped, Sofi tends to worry above all about **how it affects the team**: is likely to go quiet for a while and to carry more than their share, because the group matters to them. It's their way of processing it, and it has its worth. Supporting Sofi means **reassuring them that the team is okay** and helping them let go of that extra weight they put on themselves, calmly and reminding them that this belongs to everyone.
If Sofi goes quiet and seems to be carrying it all, a "the team is okay, we'll sort this out together" helps more than asking them to talk right away.
# What lights them up
What usually lights Sofi up most is **feeling that the group is doing well and being able to be a reliable support**. A calm atmosphere, with no surprises, and knowing that others count on their support, make Sofi feel right at home. Feeling trusted is often their best fuel.
An "I know I can count on you" will probably reach Sofi more than loud praise.
# Words that connect (and the ones that jar)
I'm counting on you for this. | Thank you for holding the group together. | Take all the time you need. | Your calm is good for everyone.
Come on, hurry up, there's no time. | We're changing everything on the fly. | If you don't like it, deal with it.
It's not a script, it's a compass for tone: what usually **reaches Sofi most is valuing their steadiness and their calm**, and what jars is rushing or abrupt changes with no warning. With that in mind, the exact words are up to you.
# Before, during and after
Three moments where a small intention changes a lot.
Give them predictability: tell them what's going to happen and what their place is. Knowing that **the ground is firm and that there's a place for their contribution** focuses and calms Sofi.
If something creates tension, instead of rushing them, **stay alongside them calmly**: slow the pace for a moment and reassure them that everything is in order.
Acknowledge **when they hold the group together**, not just the result. A supportive profile is usually reached more by a "you were everyone's support" than by general praise.
If there's a new activity on Saturday, telling them "you'll know what comes next at every moment" turns nerves into calm.
# A reset that works
When they get frustrated, noise and tension usually weigh on Sofi, and what they need is to get their calm back. What **usually helps most is easing off and returning to the familiar**: a quiet moment, a familiar routine, the reassurance that everything is in order. With a calm atmosphere, they usually settle back in.
Instead of "come on, keep going now", a "take a minute, there's no rush" usually gets things back on track much better.
# Beyond the sport
This way of supporting **doesn't live only in sport**. It tends to show up when Sofi looks after a sibling, calms an argument, or is the one everyone turns to when something gets tricky. Seeing it in those moments helps you understand that their calm isn't passivity: it's **their way of being in the world**.
If at home they're the one who brings peace and whom everyone leans on, you're seeing the same engine that drives Sofi in the activity.
